one_line_takeaway: Distilling CWE repair knowledge into compact (Action, Key Element) patterns and injecting matcher-selected ones into a fine-tuned code LLM lifts exact-match vulnerability-repair accuracy to a state-of-the-art 31.5% on PrimeVul (+7.6 pts) and generalizes to Java, though it still assumes oracle bug localization and CWE labels.
key_finding: On PrimeVul (C/C++), VulKey (StarCoder backbone) reaches 31.5% EM, a +7.6 absolute point gain over the strongest baseline (StarCoder fine-tuned on bug-fix + vul-fix data, 23.9%), i.e. a 32% relative gain from expert-knowledge integration. It outperforms task-specific and closed-source baselines by wide margins: 8.5x VulRepair (3.7%), 3.6x VulMaster (8.7%), 1.4x NTR (22.8%), and 3x GPT-5 (10.6%; GPT-4.1 was 10.1%). Manual semantic-equivalence assessment agrees with EM: 43.2% of VulKey patches judged semantically equivalent vs 36.5% for the base StarCoder (n=435). Per-CWE, VulKey improved every one of the 15 CWE categories whose rate changed, with large jumps for CWE-415 Double Free (20.0%->60.0%) and CWE-416 Use-After-Free (10.34%->20.69%). Ablations confirm all three levels matter: dropping CWE type falls to 26.4%, dropping Key Element to 27.6%, dropping Action to 27.1% (vs 31.5% full). Cross-language, on the Java Vul4J benchmark VulKey achieves 18 successful repairs, beating NTR (11), VulMaster (9) and VulRepair (4).
core_contribution: VulKey is an LLM-based Automated Vulnerability Repair (AVR) framework whose central, own contribution is a three-level hierarchical abstraction of security expert knowledge -- (CWE type, syntactic Action, semantic Key Element) -- used to guide patch generation. The authors argue that prior AVR handles domain knowledge poorly: VulRepair/VRepair merely concatenate the CWE-ID as a prefix (shown to give negligible or slightly negative benefit), VulMaster injects rigid concrete CWE-website examples that fail to generalize, and template-based APR (NTR/TBar, cited prior work) uses purely syntactic actions that are too coarse to capture security semantics. VulKey's fix is to distill each historical vul-fix diff into a compact (Action, Key Element) pattern where the Action names the edit operator (e.g. Insert Range Checker, Insert Method Invocation Expression) and the Key Element is a short verbatim snippet from the added '+' lines that instantiates the actual security mechanism (e.g. mutex_lock vs get_net for CWE-416, distinguishing synchronization-primitive fixes from reference-counting fixes). It is realized as a two-stage pipeline: (1) an expert-knowledge matcher (CodeT5p-220M) predicts top-k patterns from CWE type + vulnerable code; (2) a progressively fine-tuned decoder-only code LLM (StarCoderBase-15B primary) generates the patch conditioned on the injected patterns. The pattern selection is deliberately decoupled from generation (patterns injected only at inference), so the matcher, action inventory, or backbone can be swapped without retraining the generator.
threat_model: This is a defensive security-engineering paper, not an attack paper. The object being mitigated is exploitable software vulnerabilities (memory-safety errors, input-validation flaws, etc.) tracked via CWE/CVE/NVD. VulKey consumes three input signals consistent with prior AVR work: (1) a vulnerable function with the buggy region marked by //bug_start//bug_end, (2) the CWE identifier/name produced by upstream detection/triage tools (CodeQL, SonarQube), and (3) historical vul-fix pairs for pattern extraction/training. Critically, the paper assumes ORACLE vulnerability metadata -- known buggy-statement localization and known correct CWE type -- and explicitly frames its results as an upper-bound evaluation, leaving robustness to noisy SAST localization/CWE signals to future work. There is no adversary model over the repair system itself (e.g. no poisoning or evasion analysis of the matcher); the security framing is about correctly eliminating a class of weakness without introducing regressions.
limitations: The absolute repair rate remains low (31.5% EM), a point the authors concede. Their root-cause analysis of 100 failures attributes them to missing cross-function/repository context (35 cases), unrecognized user-defined/project-specific APIs (24), exact string/constant sensitivity (10), and complex logical conditions (9). The strongest external-validity caveat is the oracle-metadata assumption: perfect buggy-statement localization and correct CWE labels are assumed, so real deployment behind imperfect SAST tools would likely degrade -- results are explicitly an upper bound. Evaluation is limited to C/C++ and Java. Rare CWE types absent from training cannot be matched by label (only partially mitigated by the code-based matcher). The EM metric under-counts correct-but-syntactically-different patches (5 failure cases were semantically equivalent). First-stage matching precision is modest (Precision@10 of 16.4% for combined Action+Key-Element, and Top-1 matched-pattern correctness is only 43% Action / 19% Key-Element), so the approach leans on the top-10 candidate breadth (Recall@10 56.8%) rather than pinpoint retrieval. Data-leakage check found only 13/435 PrimeVul test samples overlapping StarCoder training data, of which VulKey fixed just 2, suggesting memorization impact is negligible.

datasets_used: PrimeVul (real-world C/C++ vulnerabilities; 5,480 vul-fix pairs, 140 CWEs; 3,789/480/435 train/val/test split; aggregates BigVul, CrossVul, CVEfixes, DiverseVul) -- primary training + evaluation benchmark
Transfer bug-fix dataset (~1M Java bug-fix pairs; 101,475 sampled by NTR, split 97,416/2,029/2,030) -- first-phase transfer-learning corpus for the generator
X1 (Java single-function vulnerability-fix dataset; 1,334 samples, 810/272/252) -- trains the Java matcher
Vul4J (35 single-hunk Java vulnerabilities drawn from the 79 reproducible vulns across 51 OSS projects) -- cross-language evaluation benchmark
quantitative_results: VulKey PrimeVul EM = 31.5% (100 samples), best baseline StarCoder+bug+vul = 23.9% -> +7.6 absolute points / +32% relative
Baseline PrimeVul EM: VulRepair 3.7%, VulMaster 8.7%, NTR 22.8%, GPT-5 10.6%, GPT-4.1 10.1% (VulKey = 8.5x/3.6x/1.4x/3x respectively)
Two-phase fine-tuning (bug->vul) EM gains on base LCMs: CodeLlama 17.0->20.4, QwenCoder 20.6->23.6, StarCoder 20.4->23.9
Ablation EM: full VulKey 31.5%; w/o CWE type 26.4%; w/o Key Element 27.6%; w/o Action 27.1%
Pattern-quality validation (n=100): extracted patterns Action-correctness 97.0%, Key-Element-correctness 93.0%, 94% clearly actionable; matched Top-10 Action-corr 80.0%, KE-corr 58.0%, L1+L2 hit 93%
First-stage matching (N=435, k=10): Action+Key-Element Recall@10 56.8% / Precision@10 16.4%; Action-only 82.3% / 42.0%; Key-Element-only 64.6% / 34.7%
Vul4J successful repairs: VulKey 18, NTR 11, VulMaster 9, VulRepair 4, Codex-12B 6; VulKey_sc StarCoder 14/18, VulKey_qc Qwen 9/16

Preliminary study -- CWE-prefix concatenation gave no benefit: StarCoderBase-15B 23.9->23.6, Qwen2.5-Coder-32B 23.2->22.5 EM; concrete-example few-shot hurt: Random 11.9/12.9, similarity-RAG 17.9/16.8 vs base 23.9/23.2
Data-leakage: 13/435 PrimeVul test samples overlap StarCoder training corpus; VulKey correctly repaired only 2 of them
baselines_compared: VulMaster (CodeT5 AVR with CWE-website example injection)
GPT-5 and GPT-4.1 (closed-source LLMs, few-shot prompting)
Base fine-tuned code LLMs: StarCoderBase-15B, Qwen2.5-Coder-32B, CodeLlama-70B, DeepSeek-Coder-V2-Lite-Base-16B
Codex-12B (Vul4J comparison, from prior reported results)
